Original intent: Check published and in-flight campaign assets across channels to ensure the forbidden language is not present and approved alternatives are used consistently.
How we are doing this: Run a channel-by-channel QA pass using a prohibited-phrase scan plus human review; check ads, landing pages, email, SDR scripts, social posts, PR drafts, webinars, and partner copy against the avoid list.
Preliminary solution: Implement this as part of the “Campaign avoid-list controls for launch campaigns” workstream. The working solution should be practical, versioned, and reviewable: create the artifact, connect it to the relevant claim/disclosure/copy-control library, assign an owner, capture evidence, and route it through legal/compliance review where required.
Primary deliverable: QA worksheet with asset ID, channel, risky phrase found, replacement used, reviewer, approval status, and evidence link.
Dependencies / inputs: Campaign asset inventory, avoid-list library, approved alternatives, owners, latest creative files.
Implementation notes: Keep the output in the same repository or operating system as the claims matrix/copy library so GTM users do not rely on stale documents. Mark each item as approved, conditional, prohibited, or pending review where relevant, and include examples that sales, marketing, partnerships, and founders can reuse.
Acceptance criteria: All in-flight and published campaign assets are checked; violations are remediated or logged as blocked; evidence is attached for each reviewed asset.

Summary: Define approved, conditional, and prohibited wording for each claim category
Description:
Hierarchy / linkage: TGTM-200 is a Task. Parent Story: TGTM-21 — Create public claims matrix for licensing, approval, ownership, returns, liquidity, security, and eligibility. Grandparent Epic: TGTM-1 — Build compliance-safe GTM claim guardrails. Task is under Story TGTM-21, which is under visible Epic TGTM-1.
Original intent: For every identified claim category, write clear guidance for what is approved, what requires conditions or qualifiers, and what is prohibited, with concise examples that can be reused by marketing and sales.



The guide covers all seven claim categories from the parent story [https://bx-estate.atlassian.net/browse/TGTM-21:|https://bx-estate.atlassian.net/browse/TGTM-21:|smart-link] 


Licensing — what you can say about regulatory status vs. overstating scope
Approval — referencing regulatory review without implying endorsement
Ownership — clarifying "economic interest" vs. implying direct title
Returns — historical data with disclaimers vs. guaranteed-returns language
Liquidity — marketplace availability with demand caveats vs. "sell anytime"
Security — verifiable platform protections vs. "unhackable" absolutes
Eligibility — KYC/jurisdictional requirements vs. "anyone can invest"


Each category has concrete example phrases for 

✅ Approved, 

⚠️ Conditional (with required qualifiers), and 

🚫 Prohibited, 



plus a "How to Use" section at the end that ties into the approval workflow being built in [https://bx-estate.atlassian.net/browse/TGTM-205.|https://bx-estate.atlassian.net/browse/TGTM-205.|smart-link]
How we are doing this: Define the wording status for each claim category: approved for use, conditional with caveats/evidence, or prohibited.
Preliminary solution: Implement this as part of the “Public claims matrix for licensing, ownership, returns, liquidity, security, and eligibility” workstream. The working solution should be practical, versioned, and reviewable: create the artifact, connect it to the relevant claim/disclosure/copy-control library, assign an owner, capture evidence, and route it through legal/compliance review where required.
Primary deliverable: Approved/conditional/prohibited wording matrix for each claim category.

Original intent: Create recurring checklist to verify VARA, DLD, DFSA/DIFC, ADGM, QFC, CMA, CBB, and other relevant status language before public use.
How we are doing this: Create a repeatable verification routine so public claims about licensing, approval, registration, regulated partners, and jurisdictions are accurate at the moment of use.
Preliminary solution: Set up a regulatory-status tracker with jurisdiction, claim type, source evidence, owner, last verified date, expiry/review date, approved wording, and change-trigger rules. Require re-verification before major campaigns and whenever status, partner, or jurisdiction assumptions change.
Primary deliverable: Regulatory-status checklist, approved jurisdiction language, evidence archive, cadence calendar, and escalation process.
Dependencies / inputs: Legal counsel, compliance owners, regulator/partner documentation, jurisdiction launch plan, content inventory, and claims matrix.
Implementation notes: Use the child Tasks as the implementation checklist. Each child Task should produce a concrete artifact that feeds this Story’s final operating asset, and each artifact should be legally/compliance reviewed before it becomes reusable GTM guidance.
Acceptance criteria: Status claims are source-backed, time-stamped, owner-assigned, and rechecked before publication or after triggering events.
Parent summary: Build compliance-safe GTM claim guardrails

Summary: Set legal review workflow for website, paid ads, PR, investor communications, and partnership material
Description:
Hierarchy / linkage: TGTM-23 is a Story. Direct parent: TGTM-1 — Build compliance-safe GTM claim guardrails. Story is directly under visible Epic TGTM-1.
Original intent: Define how GTM assets move from draft to compliance review to approval before publication.
How we are doing this: Transform ad hoc legal review into a structured workflow that routes high-risk assets to the right reviewers before launch while preserving speed for low-risk updates.
Preliminary solution: Create an intake form, risk tiering rules, reviewer decision rights, SLA expectations, revision/resubmission loop, exception handling, approval log, and retention requirements for every public GTM asset.
Primary deliverable: Draft-to-approval workflow, submission checklist, reviewer RACI, SLA model, exception path, and approval audit log.
Dependencies / inputs: Asset types, launch calendar, claim categories, legal/compliance stakeholder availability, workflow tool, and retention policy.
Implementation notes: Use the child Tasks as the implementation checklist. Each child Task should produce a concrete artifact that feeds this Story’s final operating asset, and each artifact should be legally/compliance reviewed before it becomes reusable GTM guidance.
Acceptance criteria: Every website, paid ad, PR, investor, and partnership asset has a defined path from draft to approval with traceable sign-off.
Parent summary: Build compliance-safe GTM claim guardrails

Summary: Build do-not-say and safer alternative copy library
Description:
Hierarchy / linkage: TGTM-22 is a Story. Direct parent: TGTM-1 — Build compliance-safe GTM claim guardrails. Story is directly under visible Epic TGTM-1.
Original intent: Turn the strategy's do-not-say list into a copy library for SDRs, paid media, website editors, founders, and partner marketers.
How we are doing this: Give every GTM channel a practical library of approved language so teams can move fast without inventing risky claims.
Preliminary solution: Create a copy library organized by risky phrase, claim category, channel, audience, approved replacement, rationale, examples, owner, approval date, and version. Include variations for SDR, paid media, website, founder messaging, and partner channels.
Primary deliverable: Versioned copy library with do-not-say entries, safer alternatives, examples, and maintenance workflow.
Dependencies / inputs: Strategy materials, messaging framework, public claims matrix, legal guidance, channel owners, and campaign examples.
Implementation notes: Use the child Tasks as the implementation checklist. Each child Task should produce a concrete artifact that feeds this Story’s final operating asset, and each artifact should be legally/compliance reviewed before it becomes reusable GTM guidance.
Acceptance criteria: GTM users can search by claim/channel and select approved alternatives without needing to rewrite compliance-sensitive language from scratch.
Parent summary: Build compliance-safe GTM claim guardrails

Summary: Create public claims matrix for licensing, approval, ownership, returns, liquidity, security, and eligibility
Description:
Hierarchy / linkage: TGTM-21 is a Story. Direct parent: TGTM-1 — Build compliance-safe GTM claim guardrails. Story is directly under visible Epic TGTM-1.
Original intent: Extract every public claim category from the strategy and define approved, conditional, and prohibited wording for website, ads, PR, sales decks, webinars, and offering pages.
How we are doing this: Build the master source of truth for what Blocks Estate can say publicly, what can only be said conditionally, and what is prohibited.
Preliminary solution: Create a claims matrix with claim category, proposed wording, approved wording, conditional wording, prohibited wording, evidence requirement, risk rating, applicable channel, jurisdiction limits, reviewer, approval date, and next review date.
Primary deliverable: Approved public claims matrix and channel mapping for website, ads, PR, decks, webinars, and offering pages.
Dependencies / inputs: Business plan, GTM strategy, offering structure, legal/compliance guidance, current regulatory status, product capabilities, and source documentation.
Implementation notes: Use the child Tasks as the implementation checklist. Each child Task should produce a concrete artifact that feeds this Story’s final operating asset, and each artifact should be legally/compliance reviewed before it becomes reusable GTM guidance.
Acceptance criteria: Every public claim category is inventoried, classified, substantiated, mapped to channels, and connected to approval/escalation rules.
Parent summary: Build compliance-safe GTM claim guardrails

Summary: Build compliance-safe GTM claim guardrails
Description:
Hierarchy / linkage: TGTM-1 is the top-level Epic for the Compliance & Trust domain in this extract.
Original intent: Create the compliance-first marketing operating system: claims matrix, do-not-say language, approval workflow, risk language, and regulatory-status verification.
How we are doing this: Build a compliance-first marketing operating system that protects speed-to-market without allowing risky, unsupported, or over-promising public claims.
Preliminary solution: Use a single governance spine: claims matrix as the source of truth, do-not-say library as the execution layer, legal review workflow as the approval gate, regulatory-status verification as the evidence control, and investor risk language as the product/onboarding control.
Primary deliverable: Integrated GTM compliance guardrail system covering claims, language, review, disclosures, evidence, approvals, and audit trails.
Dependencies / inputs: Marketing strategy, launch calendar, website/campaign assets, investor onboarding flow, legal/compliance review, product roadmap, jurisdiction status, and partner/regulator evidence.
Implementation notes: Treat this Epic as the governance layer for public GTM claims. It should not slow the team down with generic legal review; it should create reusable claim rules, approved copy, status evidence, and clear routing so campaign owners know what can be launched, what needs review, and what must not be said.
Acceptance criteria: The GTM team has approved language, prohibited language, review workflows, risk disclosures, status checks, and audit trails before launch-critical materials are published.
